name: ADHD
description: Layout feels like chasing thoughts across a busy room. Visual noise, unclear pacing and overlapping stimuli demand executive function at every scroll. Simplicity becomes permission to stay.

checks.message: Moving or scrolling text pulls focus involuntarily. For someone with ADHD, it can make the rest of the page disappear. Remove or replace with static content.
checks.technical: Avoid <marquee> or CSS scroll animations on text. These create involuntary attention capture and disrupt reading flow.

checks.message: Autoplaying media hijacks attention before the user chooses to engage. This can derail the entire session. Add a play button instead.
checks.technical: Do not use autoplay on video or audio elements. Require explicit user interaction to start media.

checks.message: Decorative animation competes with content for attention. For someone with ADHD, motion on the periphery is hard to ignore. Reserve animation for meaningful transitions.
checks.technical: Limit animation to purposeful state changes. Wrap decorative animations in a prefers-reduced-motion media query.

checks.message: Placeholder text disappears when typing begins. For someone with ADHD, losing the instruction mid-task can cause confusion or a restart. Use a visible label instead.
checks.technical: Do not rely on placeholder as the sole label for an input. Use a persistent <label> element above or beside the field.

checks.message: Infinite scroll removes natural stopping points. Without them, it becomes hard to disengage. Consider pagination or a clear 'load more' boundary.
checks.technical: Avoid infinite scroll patterns. Use paginated navigation or an explicit load trigger to create cognitive rest points.

checks.message: Modals interrupt flow without warning. If the user wasn't expecting a new layer, it can feel disorienting. Keep modal use minimal and always provide a clear close action.
checks.technical: Ensure modals have a visible close button, trap focus correctly, and return focus to the trigger element on close.

checks.message: Timed countdowns or session expiry warnings create urgency that can spike anxiety and derail concentration. Give users control over their own pace.
checks.technical: Avoid time-limited interactions where possible. If a timeout is required, give at least 20 seconds of warning and an option to extend.

checks.message: Tooltips that appear only on hover can vanish before the user has finished reading. Consider inline help text that stays visible.
checks.technical: Supplement hover tooltips with persistent help text or aria-describedby references that do not disappear on focus loss.
